Description: Construct complex structs step by step using method chaining, often with consumed self for type-state enforcement
When To Use: When a struct has many optional fields; when you want compile-time guarantees of required fields via type-state
When Not To Use: When a struct has few fields and can use a simple constructor or Default
Anti-Pattern: 8080).max_connections(50).build();"
column14: logger: None } // Too many positional fields"

Description: Wrap a type in a single-field tuple struct to create a distinct type with zero runtime cost, enabling trait implementations and type safety
When To Use: When you need type safety for primitive types; when implementing external traits on external types; for unit conversions
When Not To Use: When the additional type adds no safety benefit; when it creates excessive boilerplate for no gain
Anti-Pattern: from: Email) { /* type-safe */ }\n// Cannot accidentally swap user_id for email"
Complexity: fn send_email(to: String, from: String) { /* can swap args */ }\n// send_email(from_str, to_str) compiles but is wrong

Description: Implement Default trait for structs to provide sensible default values, often combined with struct update syntax
When To Use: When a struct has obvious default values; when you want partial initialization with ..Default::default()
When Not To Use: When there are no sensible defaults or all fields must be explicitly set
Anti-Pattern: \n            port: 8080

Description: Implement From<T> to define type conversions that enable the Into<T> trait automatically, providing ergonomic type conversion
When To Use: When converting between related types is natural and lossless; when you want .into() ergonomics in function arguments
When Not To Use: When conversion is lossy or fallible (use TryFrom instead); when conversion semantics are ambiguous
Complexity: let f = Fahrenheit(celsius.0 * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0); // manual conversion everywhere\n// no type safety, easy to get formula wrong

Description: Use different types to represent different states of an object, making invalid state transitions a compile-time error
When To Use: When you need compile-time enforcement of state machine transitions; for protocol implementations; for builder patterns with required fields
When Not To Use: When states are dynamic or determined at runtime; when the number of states is large

Description: Tie resource lifecycle to variable scope using Drop trait; resources are automatically cleaned up when the owner goes out of scope
When To Use: Always for resources that need cleanup (files, locks, connections); for implementing guard patterns
When Not To Use: When the resource has no cleanup requirements; when you need explicit control over cleanup timing (use ManuallyDrop)

Description: Implement Deref to allow smart pointers and wrapper types to be used transparently as references to their inner type
When To Use: For smart pointers (Box, Rc, Arc); for newtype wrappers that should delegate method calls; for string-like types
When Not To Use: When Deref would be surprising or misleading; when the wrapper is not semantically a pointer to the inner value
Performance Impact: {name}\"\"); }\nlet s = MyString(\"\"world\"\".into());\ngreet(&s.0); // manual access to inner field everywhere"

Description: Use trait objects (Box<dyn Trait>) for runtime polymorphism when you need a collection of heterogeneous types implementing the same trait
When To Use: When you need heterogeneous collections; when the concrete type is determined at runtime; for plugin systems
When Not To Use: When static dispatch (generics) suffices; in hot paths where vtable overhead matters; when the trait is not object-safe
Anti-Pattern: s.area()); }"
Complexity: enum ShapeEnum { Circle(Circle), Rect(Rect) }\n// must update enum + every match arm when adding a new shape\n// not extensible by downstream users

Description: Use an enum with variants instead of trait objects for a closed set of types, enabling static dispatch with match
When To Use: When the set of types is known and closed; when you need pattern matching; when performance matters (avoids vtable)
When Not To Use: When the set of types must be extensible by consumers; when there are many variants that make match arms unwieldy
Anti-Pattern: s.area()); }"
Complexity: let shapes: Vec<Box<dyn Shape>> = ...; // heap allocation + vtable overhead for a closed set of types

Description: Define pluggable behavior using generic type parameters with trait bounds for compile-time polymorphism
When To Use: When behavior needs to be swappable and performance matters; when the concrete type is known at compile time
When Not To Use: When you need runtime polymorphism (use trait objects instead); when it causes excessive monomorphization bloat

Description: Compose lazy iterator adapters to build data processing pipelines that are evaluated on demand
When To Use: For data transformation pipelines; when processing collections functionally; when you need lazy evaluation
When Not To Use: When the pipeline is trivially simple; when you need indexed access during transformation
Code Example:
let names: Vec<String> = users
    .iter()
    .filter(|u| u.active)
    .map(|u| u.name.to_uppercase())
    .take(10)
    .collect();

let sum: i64 = data
    .iter()
    .filter_map(|line| line.parse::<i64>().ok())
    .sum();

Description: Encapsulate operations as closures or trait objects for deferred execution, undo/redo, or callback registration
When To Use: When you need to store operations for later execution; for event handlers; for undo stacks
When Not To Use: When a simple function call suffices; when the closure captures too much state

Description: Use channels (std::sync::mpsc or crossbeam) for decoupled event notification between threads
When To Use: For cross-thread event systems; when producers and consumers run at different speeds; for pub/sub messaging
When Not To Use: When a simple callback suffices; when events are within a single thread and no decoupling is needed
Anti-Pattern: \n            Event::OrderPlaced(id) => println!(\"\"Order: {id}\"\")
Performance Impact: static mut CALLBACKS: Vec<fn(Event)> = Vec::new();\n// global mutable state, data races, no Send/Sync

Description: Use thiserror to derive Error implementations for domain error enums with zero boilerplate
When To Use: For library errors; when you need structured error types with Display and Error impls; for domain boundaries
When Not To Use: For application-level code where anyhow is simpler; when you do not need typed error matching
Anti-Pattern: \n    #[error(\"\"database error: {0}\"\")]\n    Database(#[from] sqlx::Error)

Description: Use anyhow::Result for application-level error handling with context chaining via .context()
When To Use: For application binaries; for CLI tools; when you do not need callers to match on specific error types
When Not To Use: For library code where callers need typed errors; when downstream code must inspect error variants

Description: Chain Option methods to handle nullable values without nested match/if-let blocks
When To Use: When transforming or combining optional values; for clean data pipeline code; when defaults are appropriate
When Not To Use: When you need complex branching logic that reads better as match; when side effects are involved
Code Example:
fn get_display_name(user: &User) -> String {
    user.nickname
        .as_ref()
        .or(user.full_name.as_ref())
        .map(|n| n.to_uppercase())
        .unwrap_or_else(|| \"Anonymous"".into())
}

let port: u16 = std::env::var(""PORT"")
    .ok()
    .and_then(|p| p.parse().ok())
    .unwrap_or(8080);"
Anti-Pattern: let port = match std::env::var(\\"PORT\"\") {\n    Ok(p) => match p.parse::<u16>() {\n        Ok(v) => v

Description: Use serde derive macros for automatic serialization and deserialization with compile-time guarantees
When To Use: For any data format conversion (JSON, YAML, TOML, MessagePack); for API request/response types; for config files
When Not To Use: When you need custom binary formats where serde overhead is unacceptable; for simple key-value parsing
Code Example:
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};

#[derive(Debug, Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct User {
    id: u64,
    name: String,
    #[serde(default)]
    active: bool,
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = \"Option::is_none"")]
    email: Option<String>

Description: Use Cow<str> or Cow<[T]> to accept either borrowed or owned data, cloning only when mutation is needed
When To Use: When a function sometimes needs to modify input and sometimes can use it as-is; for avoiding unnecessary clones in parsers
When Not To Use: When you always need owned data; when the API complexity is not worth the performance gain

Description: Provide mutable access to data inside an immutable reference using runtime borrow checking (RefCell) or copy semantics (Cell)
When To Use: When shared ownership requires mutation; in tree structures; for caching inside immutable structs
When Not To Use: When you can restructure code to use &mut directly; in multithreaded code (use Mutex/RwLock instead)

Description: Combine Arc (atomic reference counting) with Mutex or RwLock for thread-safe shared mutable state
When To Use: When multiple threads need to read and write shared data; for shared caches and counters
When Not To Use: When you can use message passing (channels) instead; for single-threaded code (use Rc+RefCell)
Anti-Pattern: *counter.lock().unwrap());"
Complexity: static mut COUNTER: u64 = 0;\n// unsafe mutable static, data race\nunsafe { COUNTER += 1; }

Description: Use async/await syntax with the Tokio runtime for non-blocking concurrent I/O operations
When To Use: For network servers; for concurrent API calls; for any I/O-bound workload requiring high concurrency
When Not To Use: For CPU-bound work (use rayon instead); for simple scripts with no concurrency needs

Description: Use rayon's parallel iterators to effortlessly parallelize CPU-bound data processing across available cores
When To Use: For CPU-bound map/filter/reduce on large collections; for embarrassingly parallel tasks; for batch processing
When Not To Use: For I/O-bound work (use async instead); for small collections where thread overhead exceeds computation cost

Description: Understand and leverage Send and Sync marker traits that the compiler uses to enforce thread safety at compile time
When To Use: When designing types for concurrent use; when wrapping FFI types; when you need to understand compiler errors about Send/Sync
When Not To Use: Rarely need manual implementation; avoid unsafe impl Send/Sync unless you truly understand the implications

Description: Define a trait that adds methods to existing types via blanket implementations, extending functionality without modifying the original type
When To Use: When you want to add utility methods to standard library or third-party types; for creating fluent APIs
When Not To Use: When the methods belong on the type itself; when few callers need the extra methods and a free function suffices

Description: Use derive macros to automatically implement common traits, reducing boilerplate and ensuring correctness
When To Use: Always derive Debug; derive Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash when appropriate; use serde derives for serialization
When Not To Use: When you need custom implementations that differ from the derived behavior; when not all fields support the trait
